comments:
Summary:
This paper investigates the calibration of modern neural networks and demonstrates that, despite improvements in classification accuracy, contemporary architectures (deeper/wider models, often trained with batch normalization and reduced weight decay) tend to be miscalibrated. The authors survey and adapt several post-hoc calibration methods to multi-class settings, introduce and advocate for a simple single-parameter temperature-scaling method, and evaluate these techniques across many vision and NLP datasets and architectures. Empirically, temperature scaling is shown to be fast, easy to implement, and often the most effective method for reducing Expected Calibration Error (ECE).

Strengths:
- The paper addresses an important and practical problem: calibrated probabilities are crucial for decision-making systems and trustworthy AI.
- The empirical study is broad and thorough: many datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR, fine-grained vision datasets, and NLP tasks), multiple architectures, and a range of calibration methods (histogram binning, isotonic regression, BBQ, Platt/matrix/vector scaling, and temperature scaling). This makes the empirical conclusions credible.
- The key finding — that a very simple temperature scaling post-hoc correction often suffices — is valuable for practitioners because it is computationally trivial and preserves accuracy.
- The paper provides useful diagnostics (reliability diagrams, ECE/MCE/NLL) and discusses how modern training practices (capacity, batch norm, weight decay) correlate with miscalibration.
- The supplementary material (derivation via entropy maximization, additional tables and plots) strengthens the exposition.

Weaknesses / Limitations:
- The work is primarily empirical and phenomenological. While the paper offers plausible intuition (e.g., NLL overfitting, reduced regularization, lower-entropy outputs), it does not provide a deep theoretical account explaining why modern architectures are miscalibrated or why miscalibration is effectively low-rank (i.e., corrected by a scalar temperature).
- The calibration evaluation relies on the standard assumption that validation and test sets come from the same distribution. The paper acknowledges this assumption but does not study robustness to dataset shift or out-of-distribution inputs, where calibration is particularly important in practice.
- ECE (and MCE) depend on binning choices and sample sizes; while the authors use ECE as primary metric and discuss sensitivity, more systematic analysis of metric robustness (and confidence intervals) would strengthen the conclusions.
- Multiclass extensions of nonparametric methods (binning/isotonic) can change predicted classes and hurt accuracy; this is noted but not explored in depth (e.g., when changing decisions is acceptable/desirable).
- Some calibration methods (matrix scaling) are seen to overfit for many-class problems; more discussion about regularization strategies for those higher-capacity calibration maps could be useful.

Suggestions and Possible Improvements:
- Include experiments under distribution shift / domain adaptation and on out-of-distribution detection, to evaluate whether temperature scaling calibrated on validation data remains reliable under realistic shifts.
- Provide more analysis explaining why temperature scaling works so well in practice (beyond the entropy maximization derivation); e.g., empirical investigation of the structure of logit changes across classes and samples, or why learned vector/matrix scaling collapses to near-constant diagonal.
- Report statistical uncertainty for ECE/MCE (e.g., via bootstrapping) to make metric comparisons more robust, especially on smaller test sets.
- Discuss implications for downstream decision-making more explicitly (e.g., how much calibration improvement affects rejection/abstention decisions, or system-level metrics).

Overall Recommendation:
This is a well-executed, well-written, and practically important paper. The main empirical message — that modern nets are often miscalibrated and that temperature scaling is a simple, effective remedy — is compelling and actionable. I recommend accepting this work (highly), with minor suggestions above to broaden and deepen some analyses in future versions.
